questions.question: If a tangent $PA$ and a tangent $PB$ from a point $P$ to a circle with centre $O$ are inclined to each other at an angle of $80^\circ$, then $\angle POA$ is equal to:

steps: In $\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$, $OA=OB$ (radii), $PA=PB$ (tangents from an external point), and $OP=OP$ (common).
Thus, $\triangle OAP \cong \triangle OBP$ by SSS congruence.
Consequently, $\angle POA = \angle POB$. Since $\angle APB = 80^\circ$, we have $\angle OPA = 40^\circ$.
In $\triangle OAP$, $\angle OAP = 90^\circ$ (radius is perpendicular to tangent).
solution.derivation: $OP \text{ bisects } \angle APB \Rightarrow \angle OPA = \frac{80^\circ}{2} = 40^\circ \\ \triangle OAP \text{ is right-angled at } A \\ \angle POA + \angle OPA + \angle OAP = 180^\circ \\ \angle POA + 40^\circ + 90^\circ = 180^\circ \\ \angle POA = 180^\circ - 130^\circ = 50^\circ$

diagram.caption: Point P outside circle with centre O and tangents PA, PB

questions.question: A circle touches all four sides of a quadrilateral $ABCD$. If $AB = 6 \text{ cm}$, $BC = 7 \text{ cm}$, and $CD = 4 \text{ cm}$, then the length of $AD$ is:

steps: For a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle, the sum of opposite sides are equal.
This is based on the theorem that lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.
